content.text: Odilitime confirmed all development work has been moved to the Eliza repository. Community members expressed interest in testing current builds. Eliza Cloud came back online during the week, though users reported issues including inability to scroll and difficulty connecting a Milady account.

A debate emerged over whether elizaOS should remain fully open source. Community member Bawss argued the AI landscape is too competitive and suggested monetizing tools or only open-sourcing older versions. Others pushed back, noting that being the foundation of a technology still holds value. A core developer later clarified that ElizaOS is not purely altruistic but aims to make money primarily through its cloud platform while also pursuing democratization of AI. IVgamer noted a commercialization partnership between Secure Blockchain Development Corp. and the Eliza Foundation already exists for enterprise-level AI agents.

A long-time community member raised strong concerns about accessibility and communication around ElizaOS products, expressing frustration that after over a year they still do not clearly understand what Eliza Cloud is. They advocated for a no-code click-based interface for non-developers and argued that branding ElizaOS as an operating system for AI agents would be more intuitive than the current Eliza Cloud branding. Multiple community members across different days echoed this suggestion, recommending the project position itself as the operating system for AI agents. Another community member responded that Eliza Cloud does already allow users to create, modify, and deploy agents without coding experience.

A community member posted a detailed fact-check regarding ElizaOS and SecureBlockchain Dev Corp, a Canadian micro-cap company that rebranded in October 2025 and acquired Agentric Solutions in February 2026. The post noted the Eliza Foundation reportedly allocated 750,000 CAD of holder funds into a 1.5 million CAD raise in April 2026, and a B2B joint development contract was announced in May 2026. The post raised concerns about circular fund flows, insider involvement, and the risk of token holders acting as exit liquidity. Separately, a community member raised questions about a reported AI16Z class action lawsuit alleging founders faked an AI agent and misled investors in a scheme valued at approximately 2.6 billion dollars, with on-chain data allegedly showing insider trading. Some community members dismissed the claims as FUD.

Community members raised questions about waifu.fun, a platform associated with the ElizaOS ecosystem. One user questioned its purpose and whether it is meant to launch trading agents. Odilitime acknowledged he did not build the platform and is still working to understand it, but mentioned plans to launch his own agent on it. A separate member warned that waifu.fun using its own token rather than ELIZAOS could dilute value and increase market skepticism, urging the team to focus on strengthening one primary token. Questions about the status of the Hyperforge project were also raised, with a moderator noting the team was last heard to be actively working on it over three weeks ago.

Scam and spam activity was persistent throughout the week across both the general discussion and coders channels. Incidents included a fake wallet migration tool instructing users to connect wallets via a third-party dApp, users selling old Solana wallets, a fake airdrop claiming a 500 dollar prize, a rugpull promotion claiming 24,000 dollars in earnings, and an unsolicited job recruitment post using at-everyone mentions. A user named Ryan also reported missing the AI16Z to ELIZAOS token migration deadline and asked whether any fallback process existed. Mini moderator Omid Sa was granted moderation powers by Odilitime to help address scammer activity after the dedicated mini mod channel was removed.

A developer opportunity was shared involving a collaboration between Intuition and MetaMask using the ERC-7710 Delegation Framework and the Intuition Knowledge Graph. Five bounty missions totaling 7500 USDC were announced: M09 a tutorial mission for 1000 USDC, M10 a Liquid Democracy proof of concept for 1000 USDC, M11 a production-ready Liquid Democracy implementation for 3000 USDC gated on M10, M12 an Intuition Skill Delegation task for 500 USDC, and M13 a Caveat Enforcers Registry for 2000 USDC. Applications can be submitted at intuition.box/missions with screening beginning June 22. A joint Twitter Space was scheduled for June 16 at 11am ET with MetaMask Dev. The poster also expressed interest in discussing a collaboration with elizaOS.

The Cron Job series episode titled The Orchestrator Awakens covering the week of May 31 to June 6, 2026 was shared, highlighting ElizaOS launching a coding workbench achieving parity with Claude Code and Codex, a production outage affecting Eliza Cloud login, the decomposition of the monolithic plugin-lifeops into seven modular plugins, and a surge of DeFAI builders joining the ecosystem.

Development focused on stabilizing the Eliza Cloud Apps platform ahead of launch. Two critical blockers remain: the release pipeline is halted due to insufficient NPM_TOKEN permissions for @elizaos/* packages (issue #8428), and the Cloud 10-user launch tracking issue (#8434) requires final infrastructure configuration. Completed work includes enabling build-from-repo functionality, activating APPS_DEPLOY_ENABLED on the arm deployment path, automating CI/CD for the headscale cloud service, standardizing Steward OAuth PKCE across all login surfaces to resolve production login failures, updating the model catalog to use healthy Cerebras defaults, hardening billing integrity through an idempotent container-billing cron job, and adjusting organization credit balance defaults to zero to stop unintended free credit allocation.

Cloud infrastructure was stabilized following a Neon-to-Railway migration. BitRouter was decommissioned and the Cloudflare Worker transitioned to serve as the primary model gateway. Round-robin load balancing was implemented for Cerebras API keys and Cloudflare KV cache support was added. Provisioning workflows were hardened with atomic failure write-backs and self-healing mechanisms. Agent cold-boot times and latency were reduced through LEAN_CHAT_PLUGINS and optimized background task handling. The orchestrator was enhanced with sub-agent nesting and MCP auto-inheritance. A vision plugin issue was resolved by replacing TensorFlow.js with a native ggml YOLOv8n detector. Android and iOS build regressions were fixed and user onboarding was streamlined with auto-skipping of runtime pickers on cloud hosts.

A critical connection pooling issue causing a too-many-clients error was resolved by transitioning to a local in-container PGlite database architecture for managed agents, significantly improving scalability and stability. Lean chat plugins were integrated to ensure compatibility with the updated managed agent architecture. The team is gathering input on transitioning to PgBouncer or Supavisor and on database sharding strategies now that connection pooling is live.

Framework hardening, UI/UX refinements, and performance optimizations were the focus on June 21. A mandatory end-to-end ship-gate was established for all plugin routes and commands. A new Retry feature was introduced to improve chat reliability, cloud-agent handoffs were made more visible, mobile rendering bugs for the cloud-handoff banner were resolved, and error messaging for rate-limited chat replies was improved. Performance work included optimizations to agent cold-boot times, fixes for sub-agent execution failures, reduced chat latency through parallelized document-recovery searches, and standardized rate-limit detection. An urgent open issue requires attention regarding on-device inference, specifically resolving a head_dim incompatibility and non-deterministic races in Mali GPU kernels for Flash Attention.
